title: Advanced Statistical Inference
subtitle: Theory and practice of modern statistical reasoning
description: A masterclass-length academic course covering the philosophical foundations, mathematical machinery, and computational methods of contemporary statistical inference.
targetAudience: Graduate students in statistics, data science, or quantitative social science
objectives: Articulate the distinctions and trade-offs between frequentist, Bayesian, and likelihoodist inference
Derive and interpret maximum likelihood estimators
Construct and diagnose Bayesian posterior distributions
Design and interpret hypothesis tests with appropriate multiple-comparison corrections
Apply bootstrap and permutation methods for non-parametric inference
Evaluate causal claims using potential outcomes and directed acyclic graphs

modules.title: Foundations of Statistical Inference
modules.description: Competing paradigms and their philosophical commitments.

lessons.title: What Inference Is For
lessons.description: The gap between data and knowledge.
lessons.content: Statistical inference is the formal study of how to reason from observed data to claims about unobserved quantities — parameters, future observations, or counterfactuals. Different paradigms answer this with different logical commitments.
keyPoints: Estimation, testing, and prediction are related but distinct tasks
Paradigm choice reflects philosophical not just technical commitments
Every inference requires assumptions that are themselves untestable

lessons.title: Frequentism
lessons.content: Frequentist inference treats parameters as fixed and focuses on procedures whose long-run behaviour has desirable properties — unbiasedness, consistency, coverage. Probability attaches to procedures, not to parameters.
keyPoints: Parameters are fixed; randomness is in the data
Coverage is a property of the interval procedure, not any specific interval
p-values measure compatibility under a null, not probability of the null

lessons.title: Bayesianism
lessons.content: Bayesian inference treats parameters as random and combines a prior distribution with observed data via Bayes' theorem. The resulting posterior directly expresses uncertainty about the parameter.
keyPoints: Prior, likelihood, and posterior form the core triplet
Conjugacy simplifies posteriors but is a mathematical convenience
Subjective priors encode pre-data information honestly

lessons.title: Likelihoodism
lessons.content: Likelihoodist inference uses the likelihood function directly, without priors or long-run frequencies. Evidence about competing hypotheses is compared via likelihood ratios.
keyPoints: The Law of Likelihood formalises the intuition
Likelihood-based intervals differ from confidence and credible intervals
Likelihoodism sits between frequentism and Bayesianism

quiz.question: In standard frequentist inference, to what does 'probability' most directly attach?
options: The prior belief of the analyst
quiz.explanation: Frequentist probability describes the long-run behaviour of procedures under hypothetical repetitions of the data-generating process.

modules.title: Maximum Likelihood Estimation
objectives: Derive the MLE for common models

lessons.title: The Likelihood Function
lessons.content: The likelihood is the joint density of the data regarded as a function of the parameter. It is not a probability distribution over the parameter, and its absolute values are not interpretable — only ratios matter.
keyPoints: Likelihood is a function of the parameter, not the data
Only likelihood ratios are meaningful
Log-likelihoods simplify both computation and theory

lessons.title: Score and Information
lessons.description: First and second derivatives of the log-likelihood.
lessons.content: The score function is the gradient of the log-likelihood; Fisher information is the expected negative Hessian. These quantities drive both the MLE's definition and its asymptotic distribution.
keyPoints: Score has expectation zero at the true parameter
Fisher information measures the curvature of the log-likelihood
Observed information is the sample analogue of Fisher information

lessons.title: Asymptotic Normality
lessons.description: Why MLEs work for large samples.
lessons.content: Under regularity conditions, the MLE is consistent and asymptotically normal with variance equal to the inverse Fisher information. The result underlies Wald, score, and likelihood-ratio tests.
keyPoints: Regularity conditions rule out boundary parameters and non-identified models
The inverse information gives the Cramér–Rao lower bound
Asymptotic coverage can fail badly in small samples

lessons.title: Computation
lessons.content: Finding the MLE numerically typically requires iterative methods. Newton–Raphson, Fisher scoring, and expectation-maximisation each suit different problem structures.
keyPoints: Newton–Raphson has quadratic local convergence but can diverge
Fisher scoring uses expected instead of observed information
EM handles missing data and latent-variable models elegantly

modules.title: Hypothesis Testing
objectives: Control error rates across many tests

lessons.title: Neyman–Pearson Framework
lessons.description: Most powerful tests for simple hypotheses.
lessons.content: The Neyman–Pearson lemma characterises uniformly most powerful tests for simple-versus-simple hypotheses via the likelihood ratio. Composite hypotheses complicate but generalise the framework.
keyPoints: Type I error is fixed; Type II error is minimised
The lemma establishes likelihood-ratio optimality
UMP tests do not always exist for composite alternatives

lessons.title: p-values Revisited
lessons.description: What they measure and what they don't.
lessons.content: A p-value is the probability under the null of observing data at least as extreme as the observed data. It is not the probability the null is true, not a measure of effect size, and not evidence of practical significance.
keyPoints: The ASA statement lists what p-values are not
Statistical significance and practical significance differ
Post-hoc comparisons invalidate nominal p-values without correction

lessons.title: Multiple Comparisons
lessons.content: When many hypotheses are tested, family-wise error rate or false discovery rate must be controlled. Procedures differ in stringency and in the error rate they bound.
keyPoints: FWER controls the probability of any Type I error
FDR controls the expected fraction of false positives among rejected
FDR-controlling procedures are more powerful when many tests are done

lessons.title: Priors
lessons.content: A prior encodes information available before the data. Conjugate priors yield analytically tractable posteriors. Reference priors attempt to minimise prior influence when no substantive information is available.
keyPoints: Conjugacy is mathematical convenience, not a substantive requirement
Improper priors can still yield proper posteriors
Sensitivity analysis is essential for all Bayesian analyses

lessons.title: Posterior Summaries
lessons.description: What to report from a posterior.
lessons.content: Posteriors are distributions, not points. Useful summaries include posterior means, medians, modes, credible intervals, and highest-posterior-density regions.
keyPoints: Credible intervals have direct probabilistic interpretation
HPD regions are the narrowest intervals at given probability
Reporting full posteriors is preferable to point estimates alone

lessons.title: MCMC Basics
lessons.content: Markov chain Monte Carlo generates samples from the posterior by constructing a Markov chain with the posterior as stationary distribution. Metropolis–Hastings uses a proposal distribution; Gibbs sampling cycles through full conditionals.
keyPoints: The stationarity of the chain requires detailed balance
Burn-in samples should be discarded
Autocorrelation reduces effective sample size

lessons.title: HMC and NUTS
lessons.content: Hamiltonian Monte Carlo uses gradient information to propose distant states efficiently. The No-U-Turn Sampler adaptively chooses trajectory length, powering Stan and modern Bayesian software.
keyPoints: HMC scales to high-dimensional parameter spaces
NUTS removes the manual tuning of trajectory length
Gradient-based samplers require continuous parameters

lessons.title: Convergence Diagnostics
lessons.description: R-hat, effective sample size, and trace plots.
lessons.content: No diagnostic proves convergence — they only detect failure. Multiple chains, R-hat, effective sample size, and visual inspection of traces together give a practical convergence check.
keyPoints: R-hat should be very close to 1
Effective sample size accounts for autocorrelation
Multiple chains from dispersed starting points expose mixing failures

quiz.question: Which diagnostic most directly assesses whether multiple MCMC chains have mixed?
quiz.explanation: R-hat compares within-chain to between-chain variance; values close to 1 indicate the chains have mixed.

lessons.title: The Bootstrap
lessons.content: The bootstrap approximates the sampling distribution of a statistic by resampling the observed data. It requires almost no parametric assumptions but assumes the sample is representative.
keyPoints: Non-parametric and parametric bootstrap differ in resampling distribution
Percentile intervals are simple but often biased
BCa intervals correct for bias and skew

lessons.title: Permutation Tests
lessons.content: Permutation tests compute the sampling distribution of a test statistic under the null by enumerating or sampling permutations of the data. They give exact p-values when exchangeability holds.
keyPoints: Exchangeability under the null is the key assumption
The number of permutations sets the finest achievable p-value
Two-sample permutation tests generalise the t-test to arbitrary statistics

lessons.title: Cross-Validation
lessons.content: Cross-validation partitions the data into folds, holding each out in turn. Its estimate of generalisation error is approximately unbiased but introduces its own variance.
keyPoints: Leave-one-out has low bias but high variance
k-fold CV balances bias and variance
Nested CV is required when model selection is also performed

lessons.title: Partial Pooling
lessons.description: Between no pooling and complete pooling.
lessons.content: Hierarchical models shrink group-level estimates toward a global mean. The amount of shrinkage is driven by the ratio of within-group to between-group variance.
keyPoints: No pooling overfits to group-level noise
Complete pooling ignores genuine group differences
Partial pooling is a principled compromise

lessons.title: Model Fitting
lessons.content: Restricted maximum likelihood is standard for frequentist multilevel models; Laplace approximations and HMC dominate the Bayesian side. All face challenges when variance components are near zero.
keyPoints: REML adjusts for fixed-effect estimation
Singular fits signal underidentified variance components
Bayesian fits handle singular cases more gracefully

lessons.title: Prior Choice for Variances
lessons.content: Default inverse-Gamma priors on variances are badly behaved near zero. Weakly informative half-Normal or half-Cauchy priors on standard deviations are now standard.
keyPoints: Inverse-Gamma priors can dominate the posterior in sparse settings
Half-Normal priors are weakly informative and well-behaved
Prior predictive checks catch problematic choices before fitting

lessons.title: Potential Outcomes
lessons.content: Each unit has a potential outcome under each treatment. The causal effect is the difference between potential outcomes — but only one is ever observed, giving rise to the fundamental problem of causal inference.
keyPoints: Causal estimands are defined before any identification strategy
SUTVA encodes non-interference and treatment consistency
Randomisation guarantees identification without further assumptions

lessons.title: DAGs and Adjustment
lessons.content: Directed acyclic graphs encode causal assumptions. The back-door criterion identifies sets of variables that, when adjusted for, block confounding paths.
keyPoints: DAGs make causal assumptions transparent and falsifiable
The back-door criterion formalises confounder adjustment
Adjusting for colliders opens rather than closes bias

lessons.title: Modern Methods
lessons.description: Matching, weighting, and doubly robust estimation.
lessons.content: Propensity score methods rebalance observational data. Doubly robust estimators remain consistent if either the outcome or the treatment model is correct, providing some protection against specification error.
keyPoints: Propensity score overlap is necessary for identification
Doubly robust methods trade off bias in both models
Sensitivity analysis quantifies unmeasured-confounder risk
